concern_reasons.note: bridge.rs uses Arc<Mutex<AgorabusPublisher>> added during scaffold to fix a Clone panic, not during a Stage-3 iteration; commit message justifies it (fix for panic! removed) but no inline comment on the lock site explains why a Mutex is safe here rather than a channel or ownership transfer.

AC1 (fleet event forwarded to NATS) is verified at the policy layer (should_forward predicate) but not at the socket layer — no end-to-end test exercises the actual UDS→NATS path against a real or mock NATS server and agorabus socket.

counter_attack.description: The loop guard in forward.rs checks BridgedPayload::is_bridged() on the agorabus→NATS path. However, if a NATS message's payload is NOT valid JSON, serde_json::from_slice returns Null (line 257 of bridge.rs). A NATS message with non-JSON bytes would be injected into agorabus as data=Null without the _wm_bridged marker, potentially causing an unexpected event. The test suite does not cover this case. The attack: send a non-JSON NATS message on wm.fleet.test and observe it injected into agorabus without a loop guard marker — technically it won't loop (the topic itself isn't bridged back) but it bypasses the expected payload structure.

falsification.deps_audit: This is the baseline commit; all deps were added at scaffold time. async-nats 0.38.0 (official NATS async Rust client, maintained by Synadia), tokio 1, sigpipe 0.1, serde 1, clap 4, tracing 0.3, anyhow 1, futures 0.3 — all well-known crates with established provenance. cargo deny bans/licenses/sources: pass. No RustSec advisories on direct deps.
falsification.drift_audit: Only one commit (iter-1 scaffold); no drift possible. All changes are motivated by the 7 MUSTs in the intent-card.
falsification.panic_audit: No unwrap()/expect()/panic!() on the happy path in src/. unwrap_or() usages in forward.rs and bridge.rs all provide explicit fallbacks (false, empty string, Null) that are safe. The single unwrap_or(Command::Run) in main.rs is correct for Option::None→default. No panic risk.
falsification.public_api_audit: Public API surface: BridgeConfig (plain struct, no invariants to violate), BridgedPayload::new (always returns a valid payload), should_forward (pure function), is_bridged (pure function), NatsTopologyConfig::render (pure string rendering). No way for a safe caller to cause UB or wrong output via API misuse. BridgedPayload::new with topic='' produces a valid (if odd) bridged payload — that's a caller responsibility, not a soundness issue.
falsification.test_audit: AC1's test_fleet_event_forwarded_to_nats tests the should_forward predicate, which correctly checks 'wm.fleet.' prefix. However the AC's English text says 'verified against an embedded/test NATS server' — the test does NOT connect to any NATS server. A malformed forward.rs that always returns true would pass AC1's test but violate AC4. AC3's loop guard tests correctly assert BridgedPayload::is_bridged() and the bridged marker field. AC5's NATS config tests parse rendered strings; they cannot verify the config actually works with a real NATS server. These are the correct scope for a unit test pass; the gap is the integration layer.
falsification.unsafe_audit: No unsafe blocks present in src/. deny_unsafe = true in Cargo.toml and the compiler enforces it. Clean.
